title: Your AI resume sounds professional. That might be the problem. | Resume Teardown
thesis: Recruiter-style teardown of an AI-polished resume that misses HubSpot, CAC, LinkedIn Ads, and lifecycle marketing.
cta: Paste the job description and check your free Signal score before you apply.

sections.label: Cold open
sections.script: Your AI resume sounds professional. That might be the problem. Today we are lovingly roasting a resume that looks professional but has the job-description equivalent of airplane mode turned on.
sections.visual: Cold open with comic red stamp, quick zoom, Signal mascot reaction, and resume/JD split screen.

sections.label: The real problem
sections.script: Most resumes are not failing because the person is unqualified. They fail because the resume says 'helped with stuff' while the job description is asking for a specific toolkit.
sections.visual: Resume on left, job description on right, mismatched terms highlighted with funny labels.

sections.label: Translate the job
sections.script: Before rewriting anything, pull out the role title, tools, responsibilities, metrics, and proof signals the job actually asks for.

sections.label: Teardown
sections.script: Now compare the resume line by line. Vague bullets like 'helped with campaigns' do not give recruiters the same signal as specific tools, scope, and outcomes.

sections.label: Fix one bullet
sections.script: The fix is not to invent experience. The fix is to say the real work clearly: what tool, what scope, what audience, what result.

sections.label: Product bridge
sections.script: Signal automates this comparison. Paste the job description, upload the resume, and see the free score first. If the gap is real, then the paid resume and cover letter bundle makes sense.
sections.visual: Free Signal score page, trust badges, and bundle CTA.

afterBullet: Cut CAC by 32% through LinkedIn Ads audience segmentation and HubSpot lead scoring.
voiceover_text: Your AI resume sounds professional. That might be the problem. Today we are lovingly roasting a resume that looks professional but has the job-description equivalent of airplane mode turned on. Most resumes are not failing because the person is unqualified. They fail because the resume says helped with stuff while the job description is asking for a specific toolkit. Before rewriting anything, pull out the role title, tools, responsibilities, metrics, and proof signals the job actually asks for. Now compare the resume line by line. Vague bullets like helped with campaigns do not give recruiters the same signal as specific tools, scope, and outcomes. The fix is not to invent experience. The fix is to say the real work clearly: what tool, what scope, what audience, what result. Signal automates this comparison. Paste the job description, upload the resume, and see the free score first. If the gap is real, then the paid resume and cover letter bundle makes sense.
